1.label: Question 1
1.scenario:
Company A is a large health and care insurance company that specialises in individual Critical Illness (CI) insurance business in Country X. In recent years, Company A has been experiencing:
(a) a reduction in new business volumes.
(b) an increased level of lapses on its in-force CI business.

i.question: Describe possible causes behind the adverse experiences mentioned above.

Reasons applicable to both (a) reduction:
Product design:
Product design may no longer meet customer needs.
The list of diseases covered may no longer be suitable, or the definitions of the diseases may no longer be suitable (Company A might be using outdated and market-inconsistent definitions).
If the product only offers guaranteed premiums, policyholders who prefer reviewable premiums will go elsewhere, and vice versa.

The product may not offer specific features which policyholders may prefer, e.g.:
Tiered benefits
Continuation / guaranteed insurability options
Children's benefit
Total permanent disability (TPD) benefit
Terminal illness benefit

Company A may offer Standalone CI but policyholders may want (and need) Accelerated CI (or vice versa).

Product pricing:
Premium rates may have become uncompetitive in comparison with the market.
This could be specific to certain age / gender / smoker status / sum assured size / occupational class groups.

Competition:
Losing business to competitors due to the increased level of competition, either due to:
More insurers writing CI business
The products offered by competitors are more attractive / suitable
Lower premium rates
Better reputation / brand image
Better customer services
Free gilts / financial incentives offered by its competitors

The increased levels of competition could be due to the position in the underwriting cycle.
If the individual CI market had become saturated, then even if there were no new competitors, competition could still have increased.
There could have been reduced marketing for the product.

Customer services:
There could have been a deterioration in the insurer's general customer services standard, such as:
Unprofessional / incompetent / rude customer services staff
Inefficient / slow customer services
Difficult and unsympathetic claims management process
Errors / inefficiencies / delays in claims handling / management

Reputation / Brand image:
Damage to reputation / brand image, which could be a result of:
High claims declinature rates
Customer complaints
Bad press coverage
Unethical practices conducted by management. e.g. investment in unethical industries
Fines by the regulator
Inappropriate advice given by sales team / mis-selling

Socio / economic trends:
The market may have lost favour in CI products, and damage to the reputation of CI insurers as a whole.
Some of the diseases may no longer be considered as critical due to medical advances.
There could have been a downturn in the economy which affected demand / ongoing needs for CI products.
There could be a loss of faith in the insurance industry as a whole.

Regulations / Other:
Changes in regulation may have taken the financial incentives away from policyholders, e.g. tax relief on premiums.
Changes in state provisions may make the CI product less of a necessity to policyholders.
If group CI had been made compulsory for employers to provide for employees, this could have reduced the demand for individual CI.
Medical advances may mean that CI insurance is needed less altogether.
A significant regulatory breach, such as losing customer data, breaching data protection requirements etc.
(a) A reduction in new business volumes:
Product pricing:
Increase in new premium rates caused by adverse experience specific to this company, such as:
Regulatory changes such as increase in reserve / capital requirements
Changes in tax position
Increases in operational expenses
Increase in costs of reinsurance / reinsurance premium rates

The premiums could be uncompetitive because of general margins in assumptions being higher.

Customer services:
Losing business because of a badly designed application/sales process, such as inefficient / onerous underwriting process.
Underperformance by sales staff / intermediaries.

Socio / economic trends:
A reduction in demand for CI products could be a result of the reduction in loan / mortgage lending if the CI products are generally used for providing protection for loans / mortgages.

Distribution channels:
There may be a shift in the distribution method for CI sales (towards a distribution channel that Company A does not use, e.g. the internet).
Distributors may not be adequately remunerated for selling Company A's CI product (so opt to sell other products instead).
Distributors may be put off by the product design / price (rather than the potential policyholders themselves).

Regulations / Other:
If Company A has tightened up its underwriting process, this might have led to more applications being declined.
(b) An increased level of lapses on in-f:
Product pricing:
Significant reduction in Company A's own premium rates on new contracts may cause lapse and re-entry.
Significant reduction in competitors' premium rates may cause lapse and purchase business elsewhere.

Product reviews:
For product with reviewable premiums, the increase in premiums may be too high following a review.
The insurer may have had to increase prices due to adverse experience or increased expenses.

Socio / economic trends:
Financial hardship encountered by existing policyholders could give rise to higher lapses, and paying premiums on their CI policies may no longer be affordable.
The default in loans / mortgages by existing policyholders removes the need for a loan / mortgage protection policy.

Distribution channels:
Increased lapses may be a result of inappropriate commission arrangements that were in place, e.g. if there is no clawback arrangements in place (so distributors might actually benefit from encouraging lapses and new sales).
Poor past sales practices might mean product sales were inappropriate for policyholders.
Financial advisers could have churned their existing business (i.e. encourage their existing customer to lapse existing policies and take out new policies) to get more commission.

ii.question:
Suggest possible actions that Company A may consider to:
(a) increase new business sales.
(b) reduce the future level of lapses on its in-force CI business.

Actions applicable to both (a) increase:
Customer services:
Improve customer services standard, which could include:
Enhanced end to end customer journey
Staff / intermediaries training
Responding to questions / requests from policyholders
Efficient and fair claims management process

Reputation / Brand image:
Improve the company's image through:
Improved customer services standard
Treating customers fairly
Dealing with customer complaints effectively
Stop conducting practices that may be considered to be unethical. e.g. investment in unethical industries
Improve financial strength / solvency position

Increase awareness of the company / needs for CI cover through social media / advertising.
(a) Increase new business sales:
Product design:
Improve product design to make the product more attractive to potential policyholders, and meet customer needs, which could include:
Adding new diseases to cover
Updating the definitions of diseases (if they are outdated or inconsistent with the market)
Offers an option of guaranteed or reviewable premium rates
Offer tiered benefits structure
Offers rider benefits
Continuation / guaranteed insurability options
Children's benefit
Permanent total disability (PTD) benefit
Terminal illness benefit

Start selling group CI as well as individual CI.

Product pricing:
Reduce premium rates, which could be achieved through:
Removing costly diseases
Removing costly guarantees (e.g. introducing reviewable premiums)
Obtaining assistance from reinsurers
Differential pricing for large and small sum assured policies
Reduction in expense loading through improved operational efficiency
Reduction in reserve and capital requirement through de-risking exercises
Reduction in the Risk Discount Rate / Required Rate of Return / Profit Margin
More stringent underwriting (so better risk classification and lower margins being required in premiums)
Including lower pricing / profit margins in assumptions.

Customer services:
Improve customer services standard, which could include efficient application / sales / underwriting process.

Competition:
Conduct market exercises to win business from its competitors, such as:
Free gifts / financial incentives to new customers
Conduct market research on customer preferences
Making use of more distribution channels / sell through new or different distribution channels.

Distribution:
Improve remuneration / commission level to distributors to encourage sales.
Expand the distribution channels, either through the existing channels (i.e. engaging with more brokers, employing more sales staff etc)
or new distribution channels such as online / internet sales etc.

Socio / economic trends:
Offer budget product or products with a lower level of premiums in the initial period to deal with the reduced demand during an economic downturn.

Regulations / Other:
Change the benefit design so that it fits with any changes in state provisions.
(b) Reduce the future level of lapses on:
Product reviews:
For CI product with reviewable premiums, consider limiting any significant increase in premiums.
Or if experience has been favourable, consider offering a reasonable level of reduction in premiums.

Customer services:
Maintain regular contact with existing customers.
Offer ongoing rewards to existing policyholders to encourage better persistency.
Conduct ongoing satisfaction survey to identify existing and potential issues.
Contact customers who have requested a surrender to outline their options and encourage them not to lapse.

Distribution:
Stop selling through distributors that have particularly high / poor lapse experience.
Only sell through reputable financial advisers.
Ensuring appropriate commission and clawback arrangements are in place with distributors.
Set up a retention team whose job involves improving retention / persistency.
Spot check new business to assess whether it has been sold appropriately.

Socio / economic trends:
Offer financial support such as premium holiday to those existing policyholders who are suffering financial hardship during an economic downturn.

Regulations / Other:
Introduce automated premium collection / direct debit facility if it is not already in place.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: Company A is a health and care insurance company that specialises in individual and group Income Protection (IP) business in Country X. Company A's product development team has proposed launching a new 'accident only' IP product that only provides regular benefit payments for a loss of earnings as a result of an accident. This type of 'accident only' IP product is not currently being offered by any of the health and care insurers in Country X, and market research conducted by the product development team indicated that an 'accident only' IP product has been very successful in a neighbouring country.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons why an 'accident only' IP product was successful in the neighbouring country.

Indicative Solution:
Because the product covers "Accident Only" claims, the premium rates are likely to be lower than a product that covers sickness claims as well, which makes the "Accident Only" product more and affordable.
In particular that the neighbouring country's population may generally be on lower incomes, so need a cheaper product.
It may meet the need more closely for some customers, if a significant proportion of the neighbouring country's population are in very good health, and/or:
Exposed to higher risk physical exercises / sporting activities / occupation.
Accident rates are generally high in the neighbouring country and hence customers really see the value for money.
There may have been successful marketing and advertisement campaign conducted by the insurers in the neighbouring country.
The neighbouring country's government may have provided support for the product which has increased awareness by citizens about the benefits of the product as a means to reduce burden on the state.
There could be regulatory requirements on employers to provide the group accident cover to their employees.
There could be requirements on individuals to take out individual accident cover on their loans / mortgages.
The product may be simple and clear to understand for customers.
There could be less underwriting involved and hence simpler sales process.
There may be state-provided IP benefits for illnesses but not accidents.
There may have been some high-profile accidents in the press that highlight the benefits of such a product.
This may be the only IP-type product available in the neighbouring country.

ii.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of an 'accident only' IP product from the perspective of the customers.

Advantages:
It may meet the exact need of the customers.
In particular those who are more exposed to the risk accident than sickness.
This will help keep the premium rates affordable for these customers, and provide a cheaper alternative for customers who could not afford a more comprehensive IP Product.
Sickness provisions may have already been covered by the state / employer, so this product will help bridge the customers' benefit gap.
The product could be easier to understand as it does not involve the definition of Sickness.
It may provide the customers with peace of mind to pursuit their sporting activities and/or higher-risk occupation.
Disadvantages:
Customer is still exposed to incapacitation from other non-accidental illnesses, which could lead to financial hardship if they fall ill
while still need to continue paying the premiums for the "Accident Only" policy.
They may regret not taking out the sickness cover when they fall ill, which could lead to further psychological stress.
The saving in premiums for not having the sickness cover may not be justifiable by the increased level of risk.
The claimants may still not be paid a claim after the accident if the incapacitation definition is not met.
It might also be difficult to prove that incapacitation was caused by an accident, e.g. someone with long-term back problems might claim that a car accident caused a current bout of incapacitation, although the car accident may not have been the main underlying cause.
The customer may not fully understand the cover and be disappointed at the point of claim if their claim is declined by not meeting the incapacitation criteria.
There could be exclusions in the policy terms relating to certain types of high-risk activities.
Policies could be mis-sold to customers due to unclear exclusions.

iii.question: Discuss the factors that Company A may consider in designing the product features for the proposed 'accident only' IP product.

Neighbouring country's 'Accident Only' p:
Company A should consider its neighbouring country's "Accident Only" product features.
Consider what factors contributed to the neighbouring country's product's success.
Company A should consider the similarities and differences between Country X and its neighbouring country, in terms of:
Size of population
Demographics
Maturity of economy
Main industries
Size of work force
Social and cultural factors
Wealth distribution
Customer Needs in Country X:
The product design should meet customer needs in Country X.
The premium frequency should be designed to fit the income pattern of customers.
For example, consider weekly premiums if a significant proportion of customers are receiving their wages on a weekly basis.
Consider conducting market research or customer survey so as to gain a firm understanding of the needs / reasonable expectations of customers in the country.
Consider conducting research including discussion with employers in the country on whether accident only product would reasonably meet their employee benefit needs.
This may be dependent on the proportion of manual / office staff employed by the employers.
Employers would want to cover their employees by providing replacement income when the employee is incapacitated to do work in general, not necessarily for accidental causes only.
Consider whether most of the demand for "Accident Only" IP cover will come from individual or group arrangements, so as to decide whether it is worth launching both individual and group "Accident Only" products.

Consider the design that would be most suitable and how the following features might need to be amended / incorporated:
Waiting period
Deferred period
Replacement ratio
Benefit payment term
Indexation / benefit escalation

Consider whether the insurer can get sufficient demand for the product in order to realise relevant economies of scale.
Consider conducting market research to determine the target market, such as its potential size
and the premium size that potential customers would reasonably expect.
Company A should also take into consideration the level of financial sophistication of customers in Country X.
Consider whether to add onerous benefit features such as guarantees.
Consider whether to offer both guaranteed and reviewable premium features to cater for different policyholder needs.
Consider whether to offer to cover all accidents, or a limited list of work-related or sport-related accidents.
The product should tie in with state benefits provided in Country X.
Consider the design of the rehabilitation benefits, in particular the differences between those required under sicknesses as opposed to injuries sustained from an accident.
Product-Fit:
Company A may want to consider how the product features of the "Accident Only" product may fit in with its existing products.
Company culture and risk policy / appetite.
Distribution / Intermediaries:
Consider whether distributors would be willing to sell the product, or see any potential problems / barriers in selling it.
Consider whether distributors will understand the product or whether additional training would be needed.
Consider whether distributors are likely to require a certain level of remuneration to sell the product and whether this would be acceptable.
Consider which distribution channels are likely to be appropriate, e.g. as a simpler product, this might open up options such as the internet.
Operational / Practical Issues:
The IT and administration system are also key considerations.
Consider whether the system used for the comprehensive IP products would still be compatible with the "Accident Only" product.
Underwriting and claims management procedures would need to be modified to reflect the accidental nature of the product, e.g. people who have had major injuries in the past may be more susceptible to incapacitation in the case of an accident (this would need to be identified from underwriting).
Claims assessment protocol for accidental causes may also need to be reviewed and tightened compared to the one on the comprehensive product.
Consider what evidence of the accident may be needed, e.g. proof that the policyholder was not acting unlawfully (e.g. speeding).
The product should be designed to be tax efficient for the company and the policyholders.
Reinsurance:
Consider whether reinsurance would be available for the new product features, such as level of cover, premium reviewability etc.
Consider the potential costs of reinsurance cover.
Consider whether any reinsurers may be able to provide expertise on the product especially if they also have exposure in the neighbouring country.
Regulations and Other Considerations:
Consider whether there are any regulatory restrictions.
e.g. product design / pricing, underwriting / claims management.

Consider if there is any product approval process.
Implications of the product features on reserving and capital requirements would need to be considered.
Professional / Industry guidelines applicable to product design / benefit features should also be considered.
Product design should aim to minimise financing requirements.
So there may be caps on benefit levels, and guarantees offered may be kept to a minimum.
Consider moral hazard as the risk characteristics of this product might include moral hazard risk.
Product design should be considered in conjunction with pricing / profitability criteria, for example:
Premiums should be sufficient to cover expected claims, plus expenses, with a margin for contingencies and a margin for profit.

Consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) sustainability relating to the design of the product, including:
Being green / low carbon emission (e.g. a paperless product / admin and claims process etc)
Having a socially responsible investment strategy
Not covering accidents caused by unethical / un environmental actions

iv.question: Discuss the factors that Company A may consider in setting the premium rates for the proposed 'accident only' IP product.

Data Availability / Morbidity Assumption:
The insurer has to consider the availability of data to use in pricing the product.
Sources of data could include:
Own data
Population data
Reinsurers' data
Industry data
Actuarial consultancies
The above data sources from the neighbouring country may also be considered.

The various external data sources would need to be adjusted to reflect the new product including adjustments for the following:
Demographics of the target market
Differences in underwriting and claims management
Differences in product features between Company A's product and the other country's product.

The product is new hence the company is unlikely to have data specific to "Accident Only" claims data.
However, the insurer is likely to have data from the comprehensive IP product. Company A could consider using the comprehensive product data and split it into accidental causes claims data and non-accidental claims data.
The accidental causes claims data could then be used for conducting an experience investigation for the derivation of claim inception, recover and mortality rates.
One key advantage of this approach is that the data would be relevant to the target market.
In particular that Company A specialises in IP business in Country X, which means it is likely to have sufficient and credible past data.
Although credibility of the data would depend on the proportion of all claims which are due to accidental causes only.
If the proportion of claims due to accidental claims is small, there are chances that data in some of the homogeneous risk groups would not be credible.
The lack of credible data in any of the homogeneous groups could be supplemented by any of the external data sources.
The insurer could consider consulting the reinsurers especially those that have exposure to the neighbouring country and get the experience from the neighbouring country.
Population statistics in the Country X on injuries and disabilities may be available from government departments.
Expenses:
Consider using Company A's own expense experience.
Consider the appropriate allowance for (one-off) development / project costs.
Need to consider expected volume of new business to derive a suitable per policy expense.
Consider how to make suitable allowance for expenses (e.g. initial expenses, claims management expenses and expense inflation).
Commission:
Consider appropriate allowance for commission level based on types of Intermediaries
and suitable commission structure. e.g. indemnity or regular commission payment.
Reserving and Capital Requirements:
Consider appropriate allowance for reserving and capital requirements.
These could be based on regulatory requirements
or internal economic capital / risk capital requirements if higher.
Lapse / Persistency:
Consider appropriate allowance for lapses.
The starting point could be based on the lapse experience of its own comprehensive IP products, and adjust appropriately for any known / expected customer behaviour for the "Accident Only" product.
Any further considerations on future trends / changes in socio / economic conditions.
Tax:
Consider appropriate allowance for company taxation on IP business.
Consider whether Company A may be able to utilise any unused tax relief if applicable.
Investment returns on assets:
Consider appropriate allowance for expected investment returns on assets backing liabilities.
Take into consideration of the nature, term and currency of the assets.
Risk Discount Rate / Required Rate of Re:
The Risk Discount Rate / Required Rate of Return should be based on Company A's risk policy / appetite
and returns / profit margins expected by its key stakeholders. e.g. shareholders, parent company, members etc.
Pricing Margins:
All the assumptions should be best estimate.
Consider whether additional margins may be added to reflect that the product is New and that Company A does not have experience in the "Accident Only" product.
The margin could be allowed for within each assumption individually where appropriate
or within the Risk Discount Rate / Required Rate of Return.
However, higher levels of margins may make the product not affordable.
Consider the costs / benefits of reinsurance.
Pricing approach:
Consider what is the most suitable approach for pricing the product.
Two approaches could be considered, namely the equation of value / formula approach or cash flow techniques.
Equation of value approach would be simple to apply and save costs and time.
However, it is likely to be too simplistic for the purpose of pricing IP products.
The cashflow approach would be more suitable to deal with the complex product features of an IP product,
and the functionality to perform sensitivity analysis of premium rates to varying assumptions.
Expected value of future premiums should equate to present value of claims, expenses and profit.
Consider what level of profit would be required.

Regulation should be considered in terms of its impact on pricing, for example:
Minimum / maximum prices that can be charged
Reserving / capital requirements and how they affect premiums
Rating factors that can be used for pricing
Other information (e.g. the results of genetic tests) that may not be allowed to be used for pricing purposes.

Consider the extent of cross-subsidies that Company A is able to include and is likely to be acceptable by the market.
Competition / Marketability / Customer E:
As Country X does not currently have any "Accident Only" IP product, there is no direct comparison locally.
Consider comparison against the "Accident Only" IP products in its neighbouring country, noting any economic and demographic differences between the two Countries.
Consider comparison of the "Accident Only" IP premiums with the premiums of its comprehensive IP products
which could help assessing whether the differences in premium rates are sufficient to make the "Accident Only" product financially more attractive to potential policyholders.
The insurer should also consider the amount of premiums that customers may reasonably be willing to pay.
Consider comparison against the premium rate derived from the pricing exercise / projections.

Consider commercial adjustments that may need to be made to make the standard premium rates (i.e. the rates directly produced by the pricing model based on the chosen pricing methodology and assumptions) of the "Accident Only" product to cater for different types of policyholder. For example:
For group IP, the potential to provide large volume discount to large corporations.
For individual IP, the potential to offer lower premium rates to policies with higher benefit amounts.
Consider whether other insurers are likely to follow suit, in which case there will be a number of competitors.
Consider the general level of the IP market (in terms of the underwriting cycle) to determine what the scope is for including margins in premium rates.

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: Company A is a large health and care insurance company that specialises in Critical Illness (CI) business. Company A's Business Strategy Director has proposed extending Company A's product range to include Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products.

i.question: Describe the differences between the product features of the CI and PMI products.

Policy / Premium term:
PMI is generally a short-term product, the premiums on PMI are generally renewable annually.
CI is generally a long-term product, the premiums on CI can be guaranteed or reviewable periodically throughout the policy term. e.g. after every 5 years.
Benefit cover:
PMI normally pays out to cover the expenses incurred during medical treatments,
typically for acute illnesses (and may generally exclude chronic and terminal illnesses).
CI is normally paid upon the diagnosis of a critical illness as set out in the policy terms
and the conditions are generally lifestyle threatening.

PMI may cover an individual risk (policyholder) or a family.
CI usually covers individual risks (policyholders)
although children's benefits might also be provided.
Claims:
PMI indemnifies the policyholder
and payments are normally paid directly to the healthcare providers.
CI normally pays a lump sum which is determined by the policyholders at outset
and is payable directly to the policyholders.

PMI allows for multiple claims during the policy term (usually a year).
Under CI, usually only a single claim is possible
unless the CI lump sum is in the form of tiered benefits, i.e. a set proportion of the total sum assured will be payable based on the severity of the critical illness.

PMI cover may be subject to limits.
For CI, claims terminate the policy (unless it is tiered).

ii.question: Suggest possible reasons behind the Business Strategy Director's proposal.

Indicative Solution:
The main reason is to increase new business volumes
and to increase profits
which could increase shareholder dividends and improve company share price.
The CI product sales may have been lower than expected in recent times.
The market may be saturated due to a lot of insurers competing in the CI product line.
The CI products may not have been performing well, due to adverse experience in:
Claims
Lapses
Expenses
Investment return

Reserving and capital requirements may be also more onerous for the CI products.
The insurer may generally be looking for growth opportunity
and the PMI market could be underserved
and there may be high competition in the other product types. e.g. Income Protection, Long Term Care.
The PMI market seems like a profitable opportunity, as PMI products are demonstrably profitable based on the financial performance of existing PMI insurers.
A wider product range could enhance the company's reputation / brand image.
The company may have spare capital that it wants to use profitably.
The distribution channels including financial intermediaries have expressed a preference to include PMI products in Company A's product range.
This may be because higher commissions are generally paid on PMI product line.
On the other hand, it is also possible that PMI commission is typically lower, making it easier to keep the product affordable (and so more popular).
The capital required to write PMI could be much lower than that for CI.
It may offer Company A diversification benefits, in terms of risk exposure / capital requirement.
Market research / Customer survey may have indicated strong demand for PMI products, this could be the result of:
PMI has recently been made compulsory (for employers to provide).
PMI becomes more attractive (for individuals / employers), e.g. through tax Incentives.
Adding a new product may also help Company A to cross-sell or bundling its products to customers.
The expense base to administer PMI products could be much lower than that for CI products.
Company A may have the opportunity to negotiate a favourable arrangement with a large hospital network, enabling it to offer competitive premium rates to potential policyholders.
There could have been changes in state provisions, leads to new business opportunities in the PMI market.
Regulations may have changed that provide incentives for insurers to provide PMI cover so as to reduce burden on state health services.
Company A's competitors may have started to provide PMI, and the Director does not want Company A to fall behind competitions.
The Director could have joined Company A from another PMI provider, with significant experience / knowledge in the PMI business.
This could be the strategic direction from Company A's parent company.

There may be synergies in selling the two products, including:
Economies of scale that might be achieved by selling more business
The spreading of (fixed) overhead costs over a larger number of policies
The potential to use the same admin system for the two different products
The potential to more fully utilise sales / underwriting / claims teams (for example, if there is spare capacity and/or expertise within them).

It might be a good time in the underwriting cycle to start selling PMI.
It could be that the Director has specific objectives, e.g. corporate objective to increase the share price, or personal objective to meet remuneration target.

iii.question:
Company A's Business Strategy Director has also proposed that instead of building its PMI market share gradually, it should seek to purchase a block of in-force PMI business from one of the existing PMI providers.

Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Business Strategy Director's proposal for purchasing a block of in-force PMI business.

Advantages:
This could accelerate Company A's penetration of the PMI market
as building a customer base from scratch could take considerably longer time, substantial resources and high costs.
There could be some good deals of in-force PMI business in the market.
Cross-selling opportunity as the new clients coming from the block of PMI business may immediately be offered other CI products.
Company A may be able to take over the systems and staff that were managing the block, which would enable an efficient business integration.

There will be savings on development / project costs, such as:
Product design
Pricing
Marketing
Establishing distribution network.

It could have come with existing arrangements with hospital network.
There will be immediate access to relevant PMI data / experience.
Company A will immediately benefit from a level of diversification (which would take time to build up if it started from scratch).
Disadvantages:
Company A may not have adequate expertise to assess and value the block of in-force PMI being purchased accurately, or identify legacy issues with the block
with the risk that the purchased book may not be as profitable as initially expected.
It may be difficult to find a suitable block of PMI business, due to existing PMI providers are not selling their business, or those that have been put up for sale are too expensive.
The takeover process could be very onerous
which typically involves multiple stakeholders including actuaries, accountants, lawyers and regulators.
The transaction costs involved could therefore be substantial.
There is always the risk that the takeover may not be approved by the regulator.
As PMI are generally annual renewable policies, the risk of non-renewal could be High.
Some of the existing customers under the PMI block business may not like Company A's brand name and will not renew their policies.
This will reduce the number of policies to distribute fixed expenses hence the expense per policy will increase more than expected.
The premium rates set by the vendor / seller of the PMI block may not be appropriate for Company A.
If Company A does not change the premium rates, it could end up making a loss.
If Company A changes the premium rates, it could lead to high non-renewal if the revised premium rate ended up being much higher the previous premium rates.
The same risks apply to any potential changes to the terms and conditions of the PMI business, more specifically:
If Company A does not change the terms and conditions, it might end up with inconsistencies between the business it is selling going forwards and the portfolio it has purchased.
On the other hand, if Company A does change the terms and conditions, it could lead to high non-renewal rates.
High non-renewal rates immediately after a takeover could damage Company A's reputation / brand image, which could have further adverse effects on attracting new PMI customers.
Reputational damage could also affect the persistency of Company A's CI business adversely, as well as adverse effect on new CI business sales.
There could be legacy issues on the block of PMI business. e.g. mis-selling, claims disputes, and there is therefore a risk that Company A is potentially inheriting reputational issues from the existing insurer.
Systems and processes will need to be able to support PMI business and may turn out to be more expensive than initially planned.
Company A may not have adequate experience in managing the PMI business, which could lead to customer complaints and reputational risk.
Staff of the target company may be given the option to transfer across to Company A but these staff may not want to.
Company A may have difficulties in recruiting staff who have experience in PMI.
There is the risk of diverting excessive resources on integrating the new PMI business, at the expense of managing the CI business.
There may be regulatory constraints and restrictions with regards to purchasing in-force insurance business in the country.
Although there are cost savings on pricing the PMI product initially, as it is an annually renewable product, Company A will still need models for pricing and reserving / capital purposes.
Any assets transferred from the selling company to company A in lieu of the liabilities being taken on by company A may be of poor quality.

iv.question:
Company B is a specialist PMI provider looking for a buyer to take over the company in its entirety. Company B has invited Company A to submit an offer.

Describe the factors that Company A may consider in order to arrive at a purchase price for Company B.

Pricing Considerations \u2013 methodolog:
PMI products are generally annually renewable, the main value of purchasing an in-force block of PMI therefore relies on existing policyholders continue to renew their policies.
A projected cashflow approach should therefore be adopted.
Projected cashflows would be discounted back by the Risk Discount Rate to arrive at an initial value
which would then be adjusted to allow for commercial and strategic considerations.
Projection time horizon:
Consider a suitable time horizon for projections.
As PMI products are annually renewable, the time horizon for projections will determine how much profits that Company A is willing to pass over to Company B
to compensate Company B for introducing future renewals / new business
by passing over the existing customers base to Company A.
As such, the time horizon should be long enough to make the offer price competitive, but not too long such that Company A is unable to recognise future profits on the PMI block within a reasonable timeframe.
Renewal rates:
Allow appropriately for renewal rates.
This should be based on Company B's experience.
It should then be adjusted for expected impact of the change in ownership, taking into consideration the relative reputations and brand images between Company A and Company B
and the impact on renewal rates on similar market transactions in the past.
Future renewal will also depend on the new business premium rates, and there should be interactions between the setting of these two assumptions.
New Business Premium Rates:
New business premium rates is an important pricing assumption.
It will have a direct impact over future renewal rates, as well as the profitability of future PMI business.
The starting point is likely to be Company B's existing premium rates, to avoid any adverse impact on renewal rates in the initial years of business acquisition.
These may then be revised to align with Company A's own pricing strategy after Company B's PMI customers have settled in with Company A.
Expenses:
This should be based on Company A's own expense experience.
Allow appropriately for transaction costs, and costs of integrating Company B's PMI business into Company A's systems.
Need to consider expected volume of renewals / new business to derive a suitable per policy expense.
Make suitable allowance for initial expenses and claims handling expenses.
Allow appropriately for expense inflation.
Commission:
As it is an existing block of business, the starting point is likely to be based on Company B's existing commission structure.
These may then be revised to align with Company A's own preferred commission structure and distribution channels after Company B's PMI customers have settled in with Company A.
Reserving and Capital Requirements:
Consider the existing outstanding claims and IBNR and IBNER allowances for the company.
Reserving and capital requirement are unlikely to be significant assumptions due to the short-term nature of PMI products, in particular that claims are typically short-tail, and premiums are payable in advance at renewal.
Consider any potential diversification benefits between the CI and PMI business.
Tax:
Allow appropriately for Company A's own tax position on PMI business.
Company A may be able to utilise any unused tax relief on its CI business to improve the competitiveness of its offer price.
Interest returns on assets:
Allow appropriately for expected investment returns on assets backing liabilities
although it is unlikely to be a significant assumption due to the short-term nature of PMI products.
Risk Discount Rate / Required Rate of Re:
The Risk Discount Rate / Required Rate of Return should be based on Company A's expected return on taking on this business, taking into consideration its internal risk policy / appetite
and inherent risks associate with Company B's PMI business.
Consider whether the company has a required hurdle rate that needs to be achieved for this type of transaction.
Overall consideration:
All the assumptions should be best estimate.
Additional margins may be added to reflect any uncertainties in the pricing assumptions.
This will depend on the level of information that Company B is willing to make available to the bidders.
Commercial Considerations:
Consider Company B's market share and ranking in the PMI business.
Consider the actual size of Company B's PMI block, relative to Company A's overall business profile.
Consider Company B's brand image / goodwill / reputation in the market, and the implications of the takeover on business renewals.
This will depend on the brand loyalty of Company B's PMI customers.
Consider why company B is selling - e.g. Company B may be willing to accept a lower price if they are trying to exit the PMI market.
Consider any potential advantages of this deal on attracting brand new PMI customers post takeover
as opposed to starting from scratch through expensive market campaign
and the expected business growth rates.
Consider the likely level of competitions from the other bidders
although any information relating to other bidders is necessarily limited, where the only information available is likely to be how many other bidders are participating in the bidding.
Consider how keen Company A's management are in terms of acquiring Company B's business.
Consider other opportunities in the market
and alternative use of capital instead of investing in PMI purchase.
The Quality of Company B's workforce may not be relevant
unless it is part of the deal to transfer existing PMI specialist staff from Company B to the successful bidder.
If the deal is such that Company A could keep all staff including some management / directors, this would lead to considerations of factors such as office location, likely motivation of the staff, quality of the systems used etc.

Consider any potential legacy issues including:
Past customer complaints
Mis-selling issues
Adverse media coverage
Regulatory intervention / penalties
Data protection breaches / loss of customer data.

Consider the types and quality of distribution channels that are used by company B.
Consider Company B's underwriting philosophy.
Consider the demographics of Company B's PMI policyholders.
Consider the potential cultural fit of Company B's PMI business with company A.
Consider any lessons learned from previous business acquisitions.
Consider the potential payment method, whether it will involve asset transfer or cash payment only.
Consider the general economic outlook and its implications on PMI business.
Any specific regulatory constraints relating to business transaction / acquisition.
Consider any existing reinsurance arrangements currently in place on Company B's PMI business, including the costs of these reinsurance arrangements and the potential cost of terminating these arrangements.
Consider cross selling opportunities.
Consider any technical expertise that Company B can provide.
Consider engaging external actuarial / professional consultants on the pricing and due diligence processes.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
Company A is a large health and care insurance company that sells a full range of health and care products including:
(a) individual standalone Critical Illness (CI) with tiered benefits.
(b) Group Income Protection (GIP).
(c) Group Private Medical Insurance and Individual Private Medical Insurance (GPMI and IPMI).

i.question:
Discuss the suitability of the various distribution channels for each of the health and care products above.
• insurance intermediaries (brokers)
• tied agents
• own salesforce
• direct marketing
• worksite marketing.

Insurance intermediaries:
Suitable because:
Brokers aim to find the contract that best meets the specific needs of customers, and they are well placed to provide specific advice to individual customer in addition to just explaining the product features.
For example, advice on 'tiered benefits' regarding individual policyholder's needs and how these might be catered for under a tiered benefit product.
Such services are likely to be expected by the brokers' client base.
Brokers are likely to sell to financially sophisticated customers
and so are able to sell complex products such as the tiered standalone CI product.
Brokers are also likely to sell to more affluent customers
and who are likely to take out policies with reasonably high CI benefits
who are less likely to be overly concerned about the level of commissions payable to the brokers as long as it is not excessive and in line with market practice.
Customers would want to be provided with options, and an independent broker would be able to provide options from various insurers.
For group products, the employer purchases group covers on behalf of the employees, so the direct customer is the employer
and the employers, in particular the large corporations, are likely to appoint a broker to help purchase their insurance cover
as the employers would prefer having insurance advisory from another established institution such as an insurance broker.

As GIP is a relatively complex product, the insurance brokers would be well placed:
To provide explanation to the employers
which may help Company A to tailor its GIP product features to suit specific needs of each individual employer.
The broker is likely to have experience dealing with companies and selling these products
and it might use this experience to provide a number of important services to the insurer, such as:
Communication with the employers
Data gathering for pricing and underwriting
Premiums collection and claims payment
Any other services that may help reduce administrative work for the insurer.

May not be suitable because:
There is a risk that the brokers may recommend the insurer who provides the highest commission, which may not be Company A's product.
The commission may be too high for the smaller companies (for group business) or individual policyholders (for individual business) seeking low benefit cover.
So the brokers are unlikely to have the smaller companies or individual policyholders seeking low benefit cover on their book.
Company A may therefore need to use other sales methods to target the smaller companies and individual policyholder seeking low benefit cover.
Tied agents:
Suitable because:
Tied agents are likely to be financial professionals who should have good understanding of the product and be able to explain the complex product features to potential policyholders.
They may typically be the employees of a bank or other similar financial institution
and CI products are suitable to provide protection to cover loans / mortgages
so there is good opportunity to sell a CI policy at the same time when the tied agent's potential customers make of a loan / mortgage application.
For group business, tied agents may already have established relationships with certain employers
where some employers may be existing customers of the financial institutions to which tied agents belong.
In particular if the employers concerned are the smaller companies or individual PMI customers who may find the cost of services provided by a specialist group insurance broker too expensive.

May not be suitable because:
The terms or commission required by tied agents could be relatively high
as they are "tied" to one, or sometimes several insurance companies. i.e. exclusive agreement normally comes with a cost.
For group business, the larger employers would prefer the services and options of insurance quotes provided by the specialist group insurance brokers.
It is therefore unlikely to be suitable for targeting the large corporations.
Own salesforce:
Suitable because:
Members of an own salesforce are usually employees of the insurance company, they should therefore have been trained to have detailed knowledge of the product.
They are therefore well placed to explain the complex CI product features to potential policyholders.
For group business, Company A's own salesforce agents may already have an established client base
who are likely to be the smaller companies and individual customers
who may find the cost of services provided by a specialist group insurance broker too expensive.

May not be suitable because:
The salesforce may be remunerated by commission or salary or a mixture of both.
If the fixed salary component of the remuneration is high, there could be less incentive to try and sell a complex product.
It is unlikely to be suitable for targeting the large corporations.
Direct marketing:
Suitable because:
Mailshots, Telephone selling and Press / Internet advertising may be used to initiate the initial sale process.
For standalone CI, comparison website may be used if the CI product features are relatively standard in the market.
For budget Individual PMI, direct marketing will be cost efficient and
Potential customers should be able to get all the product features for simple Individual PMI product.
It might be suitable for a fairly standard (i.e. consistent with other products available in the market) individual PMI.

May not be suitable because:
If product is complex, direct marketing may not provide all the salient details for the customer to fully understand.
The tiered standalone CI product could be too complex for direct marketing.
The underwriting process for CI product is likely to be too complex for the end-to-end sales process to be completed solely using direct marketing.
In particular for applicants with specific needs, such as health issues / high sum assured.

It is unlikely to be suitable for targeting employers who are the direct customers (so may not be suitable for group business).
For comprehensive Individual PMI, the product is complex and direct marketing may not provide all the salient details for the customer to fully understand.
The detailed level of claims processing required by the product is difficult on direct marketing.
Worksite marketing:
Suitable because:
This may be suitable if the workforce of the employers to which Company A is given the permission to target are sophisticated.
The working population might be an appropriate target market for individual products such as individual CI and individual PMI.
For budget Individual PMI, direct marketing will be cost efficient and, potential customers should be able to get all the product features for simple Individual PMI product.

May not be suitable because:
This method may only be suitable for certain employers with sophisticated workforce, or the sophisticated section of the workforce within the employers.
Some products would be too complex to explain to the entire workforce of an employer
and it would be challenging to ensure that they all understand the product features adequately for them to fully understand and make the appropriate decision.
Such products may include tiered benefits standalone CI
and a comprehensive Individual PMI product.
It will not be suitable to try to sell individual PMI products to a workforce that is already covered by a group PMI policy.
It is unlikely to be suitable for targeting employers who are the direct customer (i.e. for group business).

ii.question:
The persistency experience of Company A's individual Critical Illness (CI) business has been deteriorating in recent years. In response to this, it has been proposed that Company A will introduce a level commission system where regular level payments will be paid to the distributor over the policy's premium paying term. This is to replace Company A's existing indemnity commission system where a lump sum payment is paid to the distributor at policy commencement.

Discuss the factors that Company A may need to consider before adopting the proposed level commission system.

Reasons for the deterioration in persist:
Company A should investigate the reasons for the deterioration in persistency Experience.
The adverse persistence experience in the early durations may be more influenced by sales practices such as commission structure.
Sales practices may not have such implications on persistency experience in later durations, which are more likely to be influenced by unsatisfactory service standards
or external factors such as economic downturn, regulatory changes etc.
Reputation may have also been affected by deteriorating capital position or credit ratings.
For CI with reviewable premiums, there could have been significant increase in premium rates following the recent premium reviews.
The trend of poor persistency could also be a market-wide problem, where CI products may be perceived as no longer meeting the customer need as a result of mis-selling scandal / adverse press and media coverage.
Advantages of the level commission syste:
Company A's indemnity commission structure may not have the clawback system in place to provide intermediaries with the incentives to keep lapse experience down.
Even if there is clawback system in place, it may not be effective to incentivise intermediaries to improve the persistency experience.
The renewal commissions may not be sufficient to provide the intermediaries with the financial incentives to maintain their policies on the book after the commission clawback period.
The regular commission system could therefore be able to address the problem if the deterioration in persistency experience was mainly within the commission clawback period.
The change is likely to promote a migration from hunters to farmers in terms of distributor behaviour.
Disadvantages of the level commission sy: Level commission is likely to be less popular with distributors as it does not meet their expenditure as well as indemnity commission.
Market standards:
Consider the standard commission structure in the market, and whether competitors generally using level commission or indemnity commission.
Investigate whether insurers using the level commission system have better persistency experience than insurers using the indemnity commission system.
Consider what the distribution channels' reaction would be with a level commission system, in particular that the general market standard is to use the indemnity commission system.
Distributors may prefer the existing system as the proposed level commission system may be perceived to increase uncertainty in terms of commission paid.
Approval from regulator may be required.
Pricing and Systems:
Premiums may need to be adjusted to reflect the level commission accordingly.
This may also mean that marketing literature has to be revised at a cost.
Systems will need to be updated to reflect the new commission system, and there may also be implications on maintenance expenses due to the additional process of paying regular commission.
Removal of indemnity commission will reduce the level of new business strain.
Level commission would match commission outgo to premium contribution and to timing of profitability more appropriately.
Need to think about whether this change will impact the business mix.
New business volumes / Profit:
Consider the likely effect on new business volumes (if the change is unpopular, new business volumes might fall).
Consider the likely effect on profits as a result of the different commission system and/or selling a different volume of business.
Consider the number of sellers to whom this applies, e.g. if most sales are through direct marketing, then the effect of changing commission structure might be small.
Consider any tax implications caused by the different commission structures as the timing of the tax relief on level commissions would be different from that on indemnity / upfront commissions.

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: A health and care insurance company that specialises in individual and group Income Protection (IP) business has seen its new business volumes for individual IP business gradually declining over recent years. The Marketing Director has suggested that the concept of 'free cover limits' used for its group IP business should also be applied to its individual IP business, where full underwriting will only be conducted on new individual IP policies where the benefit amount chosen by the policyholders exceeds the 'free cover limit' for individual IP business.

i.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Marketing Director's proposal from the perspective of the health and care insurance company.

Advantages:
There is the potential of increase in sales for smaller sum assured size policies,
Because of the simpler sales process.
If competitors are not currently offering this, it could be a unique selling point / product feature.
There could be significant savings in underwriting costs.
A more efficient sales process is likely to be welcomed by the intermediaries and policyholders.
The potential increase in sales could also improve economies of scales, as there would be larger volume of business to spread overhead costs.
This may in turn improve the profitability of the company.
As an alternative to increased profits, lower expenses could result in lower premiums, which might further increase new business volumes and hence profits.
Improved customer experience may help improve persistency experience.
The innovation may also enhance the company's reputation / brand image in the market, which may help the sales of the company's other products.
If the level of "Free Cover Limit" has been chosen appropriately, the additional underwriting risk could be minimal.
It might be possible for the insurer to choose "Free Cover Limits" that are appropriate because it has experience from its group business, and it may be straightforward to apply the changes on systems and literature because of this.
There could be faster processing of claims for smaller benefit policies.
Disadvantages:
There could be anti-selection risk
in particular that the level of "Free Cover Limit" is set too high, or if other insurers are not offering this.
If the company tries to allow for anti-selection risk in pricing, this could push the premium rates upwards
leading to healthier lives seeking their cover with other insurers and further exacerbates the anti-selection risk.
The lack of underwriting could lead to inadequate premium rates being charged.

The deterioration in morbidity experience could lead to increased reserving and capital requirements, leading to:
Reduction in profit
Intervention from regulators
Damage to the company's reputation / brand image
which could cause further adverse effects on future sales.

On the other hand, if the level of "Free Cover Limit" is set too low it may not have the desired impact on increasing new business sales.
Policyholders might deliberately 'play the system' and select benefits just below the "Free Cover Limit".
The business profile may change as a result as this could lead to higher proportion of smaller size policies.
But the overhead expenses remain the same, which means more policies would need to be written to cover the relatively lower contributions towards fixed expenses by the smaller size policies.
There could be issues in finding suitable reinsurance cover.
Renegotiate with reinsurer for revised terms may result in more expensive reinsurance premium rates.
There could be increased new business strain if the new product is popular.
If the new feature does not increase sales sufficiently, development / project costs may not be recouped.
If group "Free Cover Limits" are not appropriate for individual business (or if the group "Free Cover Limits" are inappropriate), then the insurer is unlikely to achieve a satisfactory result.
Depending on the needs of customers, this approach might bring the insurer in-line with competitors which could lead to it losing its unique selling point (USP), or out-of-line with competitions which could lead to it losing sales because the concept of "Free Cover Limits" is not accepted by the market.

ii.question:
The company has decided to adopt a 'free cover limit' system for all its future new individual IP policies.

Suggest possible actions that the company may consider in order to address the disadvantages identified in part (i) above.

Inappropriate level of 'Free Cover Limit:
One key action is to ensure that the chosen "Free Cover Limit" is at the appropriate level.
It should be at a level that will attach more new business, but also serves the purpose of mitigating the risk of the company being selected against.
Perform analysis of the average size of policies typically written by the company
and compare that with the rest of the market.
The company may also consider offering different levels of "Free Cover Limit" to different risk groups, such as age bands and occupational classes.
The company may consider a more tiered system, where there is a "Free Cover Limit" below which no underwriting is done, followed by another (higher) "Free Cover Limit" below which a small amount is done; this would:
Reduce the impact of setting the (single) "Free Cover Limit" at the wrong level or having an 'all-or-nothing' system.

The company may improve the product features to address potential selection risks, such as:
Longer waiting period
Longer deferred period
Extend the list of exclusions for pre-existing conditions
Reduce the level of replacement ratio
Remove other options to reduce risk generally.

Consider applying indexation to the "Free Cover Limit" to ensure it remain appropriate over time.
Pricing and Underwriting risks:
Consider the potential impact on morbidity experience and update the pricing assumptions according.
Factor any saving on underwriting costs in pricing.
Consider the potential implications on business profile, in particular the split between smaller size policies and larger policies
and derivation of the per policy maintenance expenses.
Consider whether guaranteed premium period should be reduced to allow the company to review premiums earlier.
Re-design the proposal form to capture additional information.
Target marketing to insure good risks.
Reinsurance:
Seek reinsurance assistance in setting the appropriate level of "Free Cover Limit" and managing pricing / underwriting risks.
Negotiate with reinsurer to incorporate the changes in reinsurance treaties / arrangements.
Other:
Ensure that sales literature is clear, particularly around the customer needs being met by the cover, so ensuring that customers choose their benefit level based on need, rather than the "Free Cover Limit".
The company could enhance risk management, specifically for risks with benefits below the "Free Cover Limit", e.g. by increasing claims underwriting, and/or
generally (e.g. through good management control systems) for all risks in order to reduce reserving and capital requirements.
